1: Chapter 23 extends the husband-wife allegory of chapter 16 to both kingdoms — Israel (Oholah) and Judah (Oholibah). The Christological trajectory of the chapter is from unfaithful wife to restored bride. The NT describes the church as the bride of Christ (Revelation 19:7-9; Ephesians 5:25-27; 2 Corinthians 11:2), and the transformation from the harlot of Rev 17 to the pure bride of Rev 19 is the exact movement that Ezekiel's two-sisters allegory anticipates.
2: Two women, daughters of one mother — the covenant community originating from the same source. Christologically, the single origin of both sisters points to the unity of the one people of God who are drawn from both Israel and the nations into Christ: "there is neither Jew nor Greek... you are all one in Christ Jesus" (Galatians 3:28). The Bridegroom does not have two brides but one — the one body of Christ drawn from both the old and new covenant communities.
3: The prostitution beginning in Egypt — the origin of the defiling alliances. Christologically, "Egypt" represents the world-system from which the covenant community is always being called out. The Exodus pattern (called out of Egypt) is the archetype of Christian conversion: "called out of darkness into his marvelous light" (1 Peter 2:9). The sin that began in Egypt is ended by the one who was himself called out of Egypt (Matthew 2:15 citing Hosea 11:1: "Out of Egypt I called my son").
4: Oholah and Oholibah: my wives: Both sisters were YHWH's wives — the covenant relationship was real with both kingdoms. That the husband is YHWH and the relationship is a marriage-covenant points to Christ as the ultimate fulfillment of the bridegroom role. Jesus explicitly identifies himself as the bridegroom: "The bridegroom is with them" (Mark 2:19-20); the parable of the ten virgins (Matthew 25:1-13) has Christ as the bridegroom for whom the church waits.
5: Oholah (Samaria) lusted after the Assyrians. The first sister's infidelity with the first imperial power. Christologically, the pattern of seeking security in human imperial power rather than in YHWH is the pattern that Christ breaks: "my kingdom is not of this world" (John 18:36). The Messiah does not arrive as an imperial force but as the servant-king (Philippians 2:6-8), refusing the worldly-power temptation that both kingdoms succumbed to.
6: Governors and commanders — desirable young men, all of them. The glamour of imperial military power as the object of covenant betrayal. Christologically, the contrast is with the Suffering Servant: "he had no form or majesty that we should look at him, no beauty that we should desire him" (Isaiah 53:2). The one Israel should have desired had no imperial glamour; the one Israel desired instead of him had every appearance of worldly power.
7: All the idols of Assyria as part of the covenant betrayal. The entanglement of political alliance with religious compromise — accepting the Assyrian gods alongside the Assyrian army. Christologically, the idols of the nations are the alternative objects of worship that compete with Christ. Paul's confrontation with Athenian idolatry (Acts 17:22-31) ends with the announcement of the one through whom YHWH will judge the world — the universal alternative to all the idols.
8: The Egyptian prostitution continued from youth — the layering of successive apostasies without abandonment of the first. The cumulative weight of covenant failure is the condition that makes a new covenant necessary. Christ comes not to patch the old system but to establish an entirely new one: "the new has come" (2 Corinthians 5:17); "he abolishes the first in order to establish the second" (Hebrews 10:9).
9: YHWH handed Oholah over to her Assyrian lovers who destroyed her. The 722 BCE destruction of Samaria as covenant judgment. Christologically, the divine handing-over is the same language used in Romans 8:32 in the opposite direction: "he who did not spare his own Son but gave him up (paredōken) for us all." The handing over of the covenant people to their enemies is reversed in the handing over of the Son for the covenant people.
10: Oholah executed — her nakedness uncovered, sons and daughters taken, herself slain with the sword. The complete covenant judgment becomes the cautionary example for her sister. Christologically, the executed covenant people is the condition that makes the resurrection pattern necessary: "unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies, it remains alone; but if it dies, it bears much fruit" (John 12:24). The death of Oholah is not the end but the pattern of dying that leads to resurrection-life.
11: Oholibah (Jerusalem) saw all this and was more corrupt than her sister. The witness to judgment that failed to produce repentance — the perversity of increasing sin in the face of observed consequences. Christologically, this is the hardening of heart that Jesus encounters in his own ministry: "though he had done so many signs before them, they still did not believe in him" (John 12:37). The hardening that Ezekiel diagnoses leads to the judgment that makes the new creation necessary.
12: Oholibah lusted after the Assyrians — governors, commanders, horsemen, all of them handsome young men. The same infatuation with imperial military power that destroyed her sister. Christologically, the imperial-power temptation of the second wilderness trial (Matthew 4:8-10) — "all these kingdoms I will give you" — is the Oholibah-temptation. Christ's refusal is the covenantal faithfulness that both sisters failed to display.
13: YHWH saw that both were defiled in the same way. The divine omniscience that misses nothing — both the public infidelity and the hidden sins are equally visible to YHWH. "Nothing is hidden that will not be made manifest" (Luke 8:17). The comprehensive divine knowledge that condemns is the same knowledge that enables the comprehensive divine grace: Christ knows every sin of the bride and loves her despite it (Rev 2-3).
14: Oholibah increased her prostitution upon seeing the carved images of Babylonian warriors on the wall. The visual desire — seeing the Babylonian murals and wanting what they represented. The lust of the eyes (1 John 2:16) as the mechanism of covenant betrayal. Christologically, Christ redirects vision: "blessed are the pure in heart, for they shall see God" (Matthew 5:8). The vision of Babylon's walls gives way to the vision of the Holy City (Revelation 21:10-27) for those whose eyes have been purified.
15: The detailed Babylonian attire — turbans, flowing robes. The visual allure of Babylonian imperial splendor. In Revelation 18:16, Babylon is described in exactly these terms: "clothed in fine linen, in purple and scarlet, adorned with gold, with jewels, and with pearls." The contrast is the bride of Christ in Revelation 19:8 clothed in fine linen that represents the righteous deeds of the saints — beautiful by the gift of grace rather than by imperial self-decoration.
16: Desire at the sight — messengers sent to Babylon. The movement from visual desire to diplomatic action. The Christological reversal: Christ comes to the spiritually destitute rather than waiting to be sent for. "While we were still sinners, Christ died for us" (Romans 5:8) — the movement is always from God toward the people, not the people sending for God as a political convenience.
17: The Babylonians coming to her bed, defiling her, then turning from her in disgust. The broken relationship — the allies-turned-disgusted follows the pattern of every idolatrous relationship. The satisfaction that the world's alliances promise but do not deliver is the background for Christ's offer: "whoever drinks of the water that I will give him will never be thirsty again" (John 4:14). The Babylonian alliance creates disgust; the divine covenant creates eternal satisfaction.
18: YHWH's soul recoiled from Oholibah as it had from Oholah. The divine emotional withdrawal — not abandonment but grief. The prophetic tradition of divine pathos (Hosea 11:8: "How can I give you up, O Ephraim?") is the background for Christ's weeping over Jerusalem (Luke 19:41-44) — the same grief that Ezekiel's YHWH experiences is embodied in the incarnate Son who weeps at the city's approaching destruction.
19: Increased prostitution recalling the days of her youth in Egypt. The regression to the original pattern — spiritual recidivism. The NT pattern of spiritual regression is addressed by the letter of Hebrews (Hebrews 6:4-6; Ezekiel 10:26-31) — the repeated warning not to fall away from Christ back to the patterns that Christ has replaced. The regression to Egyptian apostasy is the paradigm for falling away from the new covenant.
20: The explicit Egyptian-lust description. The raw animal dimension of the Egypt-desire — the purely physical, non-covenantal, non-personal character of the alternative. Christologically, the contrast is with the covenant love that Christ offers: intimate, personal, faithful — not the anonymous power-lust that Egypt represented but the named, face-to-face knowledge of John 10:3 (the shepherd "calls his own sheep by name").
21: The reviving of the lewdness of youth — the return to the beginning of the cycle. The spiritual return to the starting point of covenant failure. Christologically, Jesus does not merely return people to their original state but transforms them: "behold, I am making all things new" (Revelation 21:5). The new covenant is not a return to the Egyptian beginning but a new creation that transcends the entire old cycle.
22: YHWH turns Oholibah's lovers against her as instruments of judgment. The lovers become the executioners — the political allies become the destroyers. Christologically, the reversal of all human alliances under divine judgment points to the one relationship that cannot be reversed: "who shall separate us from the love of Christ?" (Romans 8:35). Where every political alliance ends in betrayal, the covenant love of Christ is indestructible.
23: The Babylonian coalition gathering against Jerusalem. The multinational character of the invading force mirrors the multinational character of the final gathering in Revelation — but there the nations gather not for judgment but for worship: "a great multitude that no one could number, from every nation, from all tribes and peoples and languages, standing before the throne and before the Lamb" (Revelation 7:9).
24: Battle equipment, chariots, wagons, the assembly of peoples — judgment by military force. The juridical framing of the military assault: the nations as YHWH's judicial instruments. Christologically, the cross is also a juridical act — the divine court passing sentence on sin through the crucifixion. But at the cross, the innocent bears the sentence of the guilty, reversing the normal judicial logic.
25: Divine jealousy and the judicial mutilation. The jealousy of YHWH for his covenant (Exodus 20:5) is the love-side of the wrath. Christologically, the divine jealousy that judges sin is the same divine love that gave the Son: the God who is "jealous" for his people is the same God who "so loved the world that he gave his only Son" (John 3:16). Jealousy and love are not opposites in the covenant but two dimensions of the same covenantal commitment.
26: Stripping of clothing and jewelry — the reversal of the bridal adornment. The stripping of Ezekiel 23 parallels the stripping of Ezekiel 16:39 and both point to Christ's stripping on the cross (Matthew 27:28, 35): the garments taken by the soldiers, the nakedness endured by the sinless one, so that the guilty might be re-robed in righteousness. "He was stripped that we might be clothed" (cf. 2 Corinthians 5:21).
27: The end of the lewdness and the cessation of longing for Egypt. The judgment's remedial purpose — ending the pattern by confronting it with ultimate consequences. Christologically, the cross has the same remedial purpose: not merely punishing sin but ending its power over those who trust Christ. "Our old self was crucified with him in order that the body of sin might be brought to nothing, so that we would no longer be enslaved to sin" (Romans 6:6).
28: Given into the hand of those she hates — the former lovers turned enemies. The complete isolation of the unfaithful bride. Christologically, the opposite: Christ, given into the hands of those who hate him (Luke 9:44), does so voluntarily and redemptively — "into your hands I commit my spirit" (Luke 23:46). The handing-over that destroys Oholibah is the handing-over that saves those Oholibah represents, because the Son takes the place of the guilty bride.
29: Taken with hatred, stripped of all earnings — total economic stripping. The reversal of all the accumulated prosperity of the monarchy period. Christologically, Christ "became poor, so that you by his poverty might become rich" (2 Corinthians 8:9) — the inverse economic transaction of the cross reverses the stripping-judgment into gift-giving.
30: The judgment because of the going-after-nations and the defilement with their idols. The covenantal accusation stated clearly: covenant infidelity with pagan nations brought covenant judgment. Christologically, the diagnostic clarity of this verse is the background for Paul's argument that all have sinned (Romans 3:23) — the catalogued infidelity of Israel and Judah demonstrates that no covenant community achieves righteousness by its own performance.
31: Walking in the way of the sister — drinking her cup. The passing of the judgment-cup from the destroyed northern kingdom to the surviving southern kingdom. Christologically, the cup of YHWH's wrath that Jerusalem must drink is the same cup Jesus faces in Gethsemane (Matthew 26:39-42). He asks if the cup can pass from him; unlike Jerusalem (who drank by necessity), he drinks voluntarily — "not my will but yours be done."
32: The cup of Samaria: deep and wide, holding much. The comprehensiveness of the judgment cup. Christologically, the depth and width of the cup Christ drank on behalf of the covenant people is the measure of his love: "the breadth and length and height and depth... the love of Christ that surpasses knowledge" (Ephesians 3:18-19). The cup's dimensions become the dimensions of grace.
33: Drunkenness and desolation — the cup of horror and destruction of Samaria. The cup-imagery as total disorientation and devastation. Christologically, Christ's "my God, my God, why have you forsaken me" (Matthew 27:46; Psalms 22:1) is the cry of one who has drunk the cup of desolation — the horror of divine abandonment experienced in full so that those for whom he drinks it need never experience it.
34: Drinking the cup, draining it, gnawing the potsherds, tearing one's own breasts. The complete consumption of judgment — nothing left untouched. Christologically, Christ's atoning work is complete: "It is finished" (tetelestai, John 19:30) — the cup entirely drained, the judgment fully absorbed. No further cup remains for those who trust him: "there is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus" (Romans 8:1).
35: "Because you forgot me and cast me behind your back, you yourself must bear your lewdness and your prostitutions." The root sin: forgetting YHWH. The covenantal failure of memory and regard. Christologically, the Lord's Supper is instituted as the counter-memorial: "do this in remembrance of me" (1 Corinthians 11:24-25) — the Eucharist as the practice of not forgetting, the regular covenantal re-remembering that Israel consistently failed to maintain.
36: YHWH commands Ezekiel to judge both sisters and declare their abominations. The combined indictment of both kingdoms as a whole. Christologically, the universal scope of the indictment — both the northern and southern kingdoms, representing all of Israel — is the background for Paul's universal conclusion: "all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God" (Romans 3:23), requiring the universal redemption available "through the redemption that is in Christ Jesus" (Romans 3:24).
37: Adultery and blood on hands — murder of children. The twin crimes: covenant infidelity and the sacrifice of covenant children. Christologically, Christ addresses both: his blood cleanses from all sin (1 John 1:7) including the sin of bloodguilt; and his atoning death provides the sacrifice that ends the need for child sacrifice — "he gave himself up as a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God" (Ephesians 5:2).
38: Defiling the sanctuary on the same day as the child sacrifice. The liturgical abomination: blood-stained hands in the sanctuary. This is the situation that the author of Hebrews addresses when describing Christ's priestly ministry: he entered not with "the blood of goats and calves but with his own blood, thus securing an eternal redemption" (Hebrews 9:12) — the sanctuary purified not by further sacrifices but by the one sufficient sacrifice.
39: Children killed then entering the sanctuary — the same day. The juxtaposition of violence and worship. Christologically, Christ unifies what Israel split: in him, the sacrifice is the entrance into the sanctuary, not a desecration of it. Hebrews 10:19-22: "we have confidence to enter the holy places by the blood of Jesus... as the great priest over the house of God."
40: Sending for men from far away — diplomatic messaging and preparation for the visiting lover-kings. The elaborate international courtship of foreign powers as covenant betrayal. Christologically, the movement is reversed: it is God who sends across the cosmic distance — "in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son" (Hebrews 1:2), "God sending his own Son in the likeness of sinful flesh" (Romans 8:3). The sending that Israel did toward foreign powers, the Father does toward the people.
41: The couch, the table spread, the incense and oil — YHWH's gifts used as preparation for foreign diplomatic encounters. The sacred gifts made into secular-diplomatic props. Christologically, the re-consecration of these gifts is the Eucharist: the bread and cup (table spread), the fragrant offering (incense) — all restored to their proper covenantal context in Christ's new-covenant meal.
42: The carefree crowd with bracelets and beautiful crowns. The festivity of the pagan diplomatic encounter. Christologically, festivity itself is not condemned but re-consecrated: the wedding feast of the Lamb (Revelation 19:9: "blessed are those who are invited to the marriage supper of the Lamb") is the ultimate feast, where the celebration is of the covenant restored rather than the covenant betrayed.
43: The worn-out woman continuing to prostitute herself. The exhaustion of covenant infidelity — the cycle that never satisfies and never ends by itself. Christologically, the Samaritan woman at the well (John 4:7-26) is the Oholah/Oholibah figure in the Gospels: five husbands and a current non-husband, the thirst that Jacob's well cannot quench. Christ offers water that ends the cycle permanently.
44: Going to the loose woman. The community pattern — everyone going to the prostitute as to a common woman. The normalization of covenant infidelity. Christologically, this normalization of sin is what Paul says Christ came to break: "you were washed, you were sanctified, you were justified in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ and by the Spirit of our God" (1 Corinthians 6:11) — the "formerly" of the community that was like Oholah and Oholibah.
45: Righteous men judging with the judgment for adulteresses and for women who shed blood. The legal-community judgment. Christologically, the community judgment of the covenant-breaking city anticipates the final judgment before the Son of Man: "when the Son of Man comes in his glory, and all the angels with him, then he will sit on his glorious throne. Before him will be gathered all the nations" (Matthew 25:31-32). The prophetic judgment of Ezekiel 23 is the temporal expression of the eschatological judgment.
46: The assembly brought up against them — terror and plunder. The divine command for the punishing assembly. Christologically, the gathered assembly of judgment in Ezekiel 23 is inverted in the NT gathering: "For where two or three are gathered in my name, there am I among them" (Matthew 18:20). The fearful assembly of judgment becomes the joyful assembly of the new covenant community gathered around Christ.
47: The stoning and sword execution of both sisters. The double death sentence of both kingdoms. Christologically, death is the sentence that Christ absorbs: "he himself bore our sins in his body on the tree" (1 Peter 2:24). The double execution of both covenant kingdoms is the double condemnation that one innocent person absorbs on the cross, freeing those who trust him from their own condemnation.
48: Removing lewdness from the land so that all women might be instructed not to do as they had done. The instructive purpose of the judgment — the lesson for the nations. Christologically, the removal of lewdness from the land is the negative form of what the sanctifying work of the Spirit accomplishes: "it is God who works in you, both to will and to work for his good pleasure" (Philippians 2:13). The instruction that judgment provides by fear, the Spirit provides by transformation.
49: Bearing the penalty of sinful idolatry — and knowing YHWH: "They shall bear the penalty of your sinful idolatry, and you shall know that I am the Lord YHWH." The closing recognition formula after the double sentence of both sisters. The bearing of penalty is what makes the knowing-YHWH possible. Christologically, Christ bears the penalty (Isaiah 53:4-5: "he has borne our griefs... he was wounded for our transgressions") so that the knowing of YHWH might be restored: "this is eternal life, that they know you, the only true God" (John 17:3). The penalty-bearing and the knowing-YHWH are causally linked through the cross.
